runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 8 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Keane and Moffitt (2005) labor supply elasticity surveys
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) on wage elasticities by income
Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on behavioral responses to taxation
runs.reasoningSummary: For higher-decile primary earners, substitution elasticities tend to be modest. Decile 8 is upper-middle income but not top earners. Literature suggests primary-earner substitution elasticities typically range 0.05–0.65, with central estimates around 0.2–0.3. Higher-income earners generally show lower elasticities than lower-income workers. I estimate a central value of 0.25 with reasonable uncertainty to reflect heterogeneity and parameter uncertainty in microsimulations.

citations: Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) labor supply review on substitution elasticities

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary-earner substitution elasticities from labor supply and taxable income literatures typically range from 0.05 to 0.5, with middle-to-high-income earners (decile 8) typically showing moderate responsiveness. The distribution reflects empirical heterogeneity and uncertainty: lower tail accounts for low-elasticity studies, median (~0.25) reflects conventional middle estimates, upper tail reflects higher responsiveness found in some behavioral and ETI studies. Decile 8 (roughly 70-80th percentile) earners are likely somewhat more elastic than lower deciles but less than top earners, supporting a moderate central estimate.

citations: Heim (2007) on labor supply elasticities for primary earners

runs.reasoningSummary: For primary earners in decile 8 (upper-middle earnings), substitution elasticities tend to be modest but non-negligible. Decile 8 workers likely have intermediate behavioral responsiveness—more constrained than high earners (deciles 9–10) but more responsive than lower-income workers. Empirical literature suggests uncompensated labor supply elasticities for prime-age workers in this range cluster around 0.1–0.4, with point estimates often near 0.2–0.3. I've centered on 0.25 with moderate spread to reflect uncertainty around the true response for this specific income group in simulation contexts.
